steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The passage says store context may 'moderate' the spacing effect — in research, moderate means to reduce or weaken. The original effect: products spaced far apart are perceived as more valuable. If store context moderates this, the spacing effect should be weaker at an inexpensive store (Forever 21) than at an upscale store (Tiffany & Co.).

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• "At both Tiffany & Co. and Forever 21, participants judged jewelry spaced far apart to be less valuable than jewelry spaced close together, but the difference in perceived value was significantly greater at Tiffany & Co. than at Forever 21.": although this describes a difference between the two stores, it reverses the original spacing effect by saying jewelry spaced far apart was judged less valuable than jewelry spaced close together. ✗
  • "When jewelry was spaced far apart, participants judged the jewelry at Tiffany & Co. to be more valuable than the jewelry at Forever 21, but when jewelry was spaced close together, participants judged the jewelry at Tiffany & Co. to be less valuable than the jewelry at Forever 21.": compares the two stores at each spacing level, but it does not show whether far-apart jewelry is more valuable than close-together jewelry within either store, so it cannot show that the spacing effect is weaker at Forever 21. ✗
  • "At Tiffany & Co., participants judged jewelry spaced far apart to be substantially more valuable than jewelry spaced close together, but at Forever 21, participants judged jewelry spaced far apart to be only slightly more valuable than jewelry spaced close together.": shows a substantial spacing effect at Tiffany & Co. but only a slight one at Forever 21 — the effect is weakened at the inexpensive store, directly supporting moderation. ✓
  • "Participants judged jewelry spaced far apart at Tiffany & Co. to be similar in value to jewelry spaced far apart at Forever 21, but participants judged jewelry spaced close together at Tiffany & Co. to be more valuable than jewelry spaced close together at Forever 21.": compares the two stores at the same spacing levels, but it does not state how far-apart jewelry compares with close-together jewelry within either store, so it does not show the needed spacing effect or its moderation. ✗
That matches the correct answer — C is correct.
